1:"$Sreact.fragment"
2:I[94919,["/_next/static/chunks/7d01d5ade1e85025.js","/_next/static/chunks/5171105a333fa2e4.js","/_next/static/chunks/e2e3d201eec6a50e.js"],"default"]
7:I[52734,["/_next/static/chunks/bc7ea62eab8f8bd7.js","/_next/static/chunks/75a90bf3213f055e.js"],"OutletBoundary"]
8:"$Sreact.suspense"
3:T45f,Each round presents an agenda with countries you need to convince. Roll dice to generate a score, then play cards to modify it — add flat bonuses, multiply your score, reroll bad outcomes, or gamble for high-risk high-reward payoffs.

Countries have unique traits and resistance values. Some require allies (e.g., Australia requires ally Italy), some have FixedDie rules (one die is locked to a specific number), and some scale their difficulty with the Doom Clock.

Between rounds, visit the Shop to spend influence on new cards. After a card is purchased, its position is randomly replaced by another card.

The four agenda levels escalate:
• Level 1 — Tutorial (Sealand)
• Level 2 — Convince 9 out of 11 countries (Pakistan, Vatican City, Vietnam, Australia, Singapore, Canada, Egypt, Mexico, Korea, North Korea, India)
• Level 3 — Convince 5 out of 6 countries (Germany, India, Saudi Arabia, Switzerland, Japan, North Korea)
• Level 4 — Convince ALL countries (China, Russia, USA, France, United Kingdom)

Some countries only reward money. Some reward both money and cards. Some only reward cards.0:{"buildId":"beaLrhS8VFXNhvAW4YJsD","rsc":["$","$1","c",{"children":[["$","$L2",null,{"game":{"slug":"big-brother","title":"Big Brother","status":"Shipped","whatIDid":["Game Design","Programming","Art Direction"],"elevatorSpell":"A retro horror adventure where a watchful eye hunts you through pixelated corridors. Outsmart its patrol routes, dodge its traps, and escape — or be caught.","highlights":["AI with Patrol → Chase → Search state machine and NavMesh pathfinding","Custom retro PS1 rendering pipeline: low-res Render Texture + 4:3 aspect ratio + VHS post-processing","Procedural multi-leg walk animation using sine-wave coordination","Multiple levels with escalating monster variants"],"featured":true,"coverImage":"/games/big-brother/Cover.png","playLink":"","trailerLink":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5LhrhZS2zyQ","videoSrc":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5LhrhZS2zyQ","videoType":"youtube","theIdea":"Big Brother is a retro horror adventure built around the feeling of being watched. You're trapped in a surveillance facility, hunted by a cyclopean monster whose glowing eye tracks your every move. The game is split across multiple levels — each introducing new monster variants and escalating the tension.\n\nThe retro PS1 aesthetic isn't just a filter — it's a design philosophy. By rendering at low resolution with VHS post-processing, I could use simpler geometry while making the visual noise itself feel oppressive and disorienting.","gameplayLoop":"Explore dark corridors → Avoid the monster's 60° field of vision → Use obstacles to break line of sight → Navigate trap-filled rooms → Find the escape route.\n\nThe monster operates on a three-state AI loop:\n• Patrol — moves between designed waypoints (Point A → B → C → D → E)\n• Chasing — triggered when alertLevel >= threshold; uses NavMesh to path toward the player; triggers death if distance < 3.5m\n• Search — activated when it loses line of sight; moves to the player's last known position, then plays a search animation before returning to patrol\n\nLevels 2 and 3 introduce new monster variants with different visual designs and behaviours, raising the stakes as the player progresses.","gameplayImages":[{"src":"/games/big-brother/FlowChart.png","alt":"AI state machine flowchart"},{"src":"/games/big-brother/Route.jpg","alt":"Monster patrol route waypoints"}],"legMovementImages":[{"src":"/games/big-brother/MonsterChasing.gif","alt":"Monster chasing the player through corridors"},{"src":"/games/big-brother/LegAnimation.png","alt":"Procedural leg animation using sine waves"}],"rightBrainNotes":"The retro PS1 visuals are achieved by decoupling the main camera's output into a low-resolution, point-filtered Render Texture, then using a secondary UI camera to project it onto the screen with a 4:3 aspect ratio and VHS post-processing — avoiding any raycast blocking issues.\n\nThe monster designs evolve across levels: Level 1 features the classic red-eyed cyclopean creature in dark corridors, while Levels 2 and 3 introduce a spider-like variant with multiple legs and a more organic, unsettling silhouette.\n\nHorror interactions include environmental destruction sequences (interpolating camera transforms between markers, playing smash sounds, deactivating obstacle roots) and one-time jumpscares triggered by swapping a screen's material and activating audio on player entry.","artImages":[{"src":"/games/big-brother/Rendering.gif","alt":"The retro PS1 rendering effect in action"},{"src":"/games/big-brother/RenderingMethod.png","alt":"Diagram of the rendering pipeline"}],"leftBrainNotes":"The monster's field of vision is 60°. When blocked by an object marked as \"obstacle,\" the monster can no longer see the player. The monsters patrol along the locations of designed waypoints.\n\nThe rendering pipeline: Main Camera (captures 3D scene in high detail) → Render Texture (downscaled resolution, e.g. 320×240) → Display/Shader (upscaled pixelation + scanlines effect).","legMovementNotes":"Leg movement uses a Movement Detection system — the script calculates moveSpeed by measuring distance traveled between frames. The Step Mechanism uses |sin(Time.time × stepFrequency)| to create bouncing steps, with multiple legs staggered by phase offsets (Leg 1: offset 0, Leg 2: offset 1.0, Leg 3: offset 2.0) to produce an alternating walking gait rather than simultaneous jumping.","techImages":[{"src":"/games/big-brother/Searching.gif","alt":"Monster searching after losing sight of player"},{"src":"/games/big-brother/Traps.gif","alt":"Trap sequences in the environment"},{"src":"/games/big-brother/TVs.gif","alt":"Surveillance TVs in the facility"}],"screenshots":[],"lessons":["What surprised me: The downscaled rendering actually hid a lot of rough geometry, turning a limitation into an advantage. The retro filter made placeholder art look intentional.","What I cut: A stamina system for sprinting — it added frustration without adding tension. The monster's chase speed already created enough pressure.","What I'd improve: Add more varied patrol routes and randomized trap placements to increase replayability."],"links":[],"credits":{"text":"Thank @黑化归来之幽默的呵呵 for the artwork you created","image":"/games/big-brother/Credits.jpg"}},"relatedGames":[{"slug":"roll-for-resolution","title":"Roll for Resolution","status":"Shipped","whatIDid":["Game Design","Programming","Art"],"elevatorSpell":"A sci-fi strategy game where you play as an alien Mediator racing to save Earth from an apocalyptic threat. Negotiate with divided global powers, draft tactical cards, manage the Doom Clock, and roll dice to overcome unique national traits and secure world peace.","highlights":["10+ card types across 4 categories: Basic Stats, Dice Manipulation, Risk & Special, Universal Mechanics","National Trait system with mathematical formulas affecting gameplay (Strict, PanicProne, FixedDie, Elitist)","Doom Clock — failed negotiations advance it; game ends when the hour hand completes a full rotation","4 agenda levels escalating from Sealand to the UN Security Council"],"featured":true,"coverImage":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Cover.png","playLink":"","trailerLink":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqc9IJIoDA0","videoSrc":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqc9IJIoDA0","videoType":"youtube","theIdea":"In this sci-fi strategy game, you play as an alien Mediator racing to save Earth from an apocalyptic threat. Negotiate with divided global powers to authorize a planetary defense shield. Draft tactical cards, manage the perilous Doom Clock, and roll dice to overcome unique national traits and secure world peace.\n\nThe humour comes from the Polandball art style — cute, round, flag-faced characters that strip away the gravitas of real diplomacy and let the absurdity of the mechanics shine. Each country has its own personality and attitude at the negotiating table.","gameplayLoop":"$3","gameplayImages":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Rule1.gif","alt":"Negotiation round in progress"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Rule2.gif","alt":"Dice rolling and card play"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Shop.gif","alt":"Card shop between rounds"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/CardReward.gif","alt":"Card and money rewards after negotiation"}],"rightBrainNotes":"Each countryball is hand-drawn with unique personality — Germany wears a hat and carries a beer, Japan looks fierce, EU is falling asleep. The crumpled paper background and pixel fonts give the HUD a \"war room\" feeling.\n\nThe negotiation screen places you across a desk from the country you're negotiating with, dice in the centre, cards along the bottom. Some cards trigger specific dialogues for certain countries — and some cards not only have special dialogue but also special effects for specific countries.\n\nThe GLOBAL COLLAPSE fail state screen shows all the countryballs in ruins on crumpled paper, with a \"Try Again\" button — reinforcing that failure is part of the loop.","artImages":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Fail.gif","alt":"GLOBAL COLLAPSE fail state screen"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Clock.gif","alt":"The Doom Clock ticking"}],"cardTypes":{"categories":[{"name":"Basic Stats","description":"• Add — Increases total base points directly. Best used before multipliers.\n• Multiply — Multiplies the final score. Best for massive bursts.","images":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/Add.png","alt":"Add"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/Multiply.png","alt":"Multiply"}]},{"name":"Dice Manipulation","description":"• FixedDie — Sets one die to a specific number. Eliminates RNG or counters enemy debuffs.\n• Reroll — Automatically triggers a dice reroll upon negotiation failure.","images":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/FixedDie.png","alt":"FixedDie"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/Reroll.png","alt":"Reroll"}]},{"name":"Risk & Special","description":"• Gamble — Multiplies score on success; halves total score on failure. High risk, high reward.\n• AnimeReverse — Inverts low dice rolls (1, 2, 3) into high numbers (6, 5, 4).\n• IgnoreTrait — Randomly disables one enemy trait for the current negotiation.","images":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/Gamble.png","alt":"Gamble"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/AnimeReverse.png","alt":"AnimeReverse"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/IgnoreTrait.png","alt":"IgnoreTrait"}]},{"name":"Universal Mechanics","description":"• Doom Cost — Consumes or reduces Doom Clock points upon use.\n• Target Tags (Special Rules) — Triggers multiplied or modified effects against countries with specific tags (e.g., NuclearOwner).","images":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/ReduceDoom.png","alt":"Reduce Doom"},{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/cards/SpecialRule.png","alt":"Special Rule"}]}]},"countriesTraits":{"description":"Core National Trait Logic:\n• Strict (Dogmatic/Selective) — Validation Layer: if (Card == Add) Value = 0; blocks additive cards\n• PanicProne (Apocalyptic Fear) — Target Calculation: Res_f = Res_b * (1 + (10 - Doom) * 0.1); resistance scales with Doom Clock\n• FixedDie (Inflexible/Stubborn) — RNG Manipulation: Dice_2 = 1; locks one die to minimum\n• Elitist (Prestige/Arrogance) — Payoff Diminishing: Mul_f = 1 + (Mul_o - 1) * 0.5; halves multiplier gains\n\nThe Doom Clock moves by different margins after negotiations fail in different countries. The game ends when the clock's hour hand completes one full rotation.","image":{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Trait.png","alt":"National trait formulas"}},"techImages":[{"src":"/games/roll-for-resolution/Agenda.png","alt":"Agenda level 1 — tutorial with Sealand"}],"screenshots":[],"lessons":["What surprised me: Players spent way more time in the Shop than expected. The card economy became the real strategic layer — not the dice rolling itself.","What I cut: A real-time negotiation timer. Turns out, giving players time to think about their card combos was more fun than pressuring them.","What I'd improve: Add a trait preview so players can plan around national modifiers before committing their cards."],"links":[]},{"slug":"f1-pit-stop-vr","title":"F1 Pit Stop VR","status":"Prototype","whatIDid":["Game Design","VR Programming","Interaction Design"],"elevatorSpell":"A rhythm-based VR pit stop simulation. Synchronize your wheel gun maneuvers with the beat to secure bolts with mechanical precision — blending professional racing maintenance with addictive rhythmic flow.","highlights":["Rhythm-based VR interaction: synchronize actions (unscrew, pull, push) with timed beats","Bilateral validation — both hands must act as a unified input source to trigger actions","Temporal thresholding — filters out high-frequency noise, requiring 50ms sustained velocity","Arc teleportation with NavMesh validation and Niagara VFX (long-distance teleportation disabled for better experience)"],"featured":true,"coverImage":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Cover.png","playLink":"","trailerLink":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7Cm7z-3aX0","videoSrc":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z7Cm7z-3aX0","videoType":"youtube","theIdea":"Experience a high-octane F1 pit stop in this rhythm-based VR simulation. Synchronize your wheel gun maneuvers with the beat to secure bolts with mechanical precision. This game blends professional racing maintenance with addictive rhythmic flow for a unique and satisfying pit crew challenge.\n\nThe core insight was that pit stops are already rhythmic — real crews drill the same sequence thousands of times until it becomes muscle memory. By mapping that to VR hand tracking with rhythm game timing, the physical gestures become the controller.","gameplayLoop":"Stand in the VR pit lane next to an F1 car. A colour-coded wheel mechanism guides your actions — the concentric rings show progress and timing windows.\n\nThree action types, each mapped to a physical gesture:\n\n1. Unscrew — Press the trigger button at the correct point; the screw flies onto the wheel gun's head.\n2. Pull — Both hands simultaneously touch the tire and lift upwards; the tire flies along a set trajectory driven by spline curves.\n3. Push — Both hands simultaneously touch the tire and push forward; the tire follows its trajectory back onto the axle.\n\nAn array-based keypoint system controls the choreography — each entry defines {MovementType, time, tolerance}, dictating what action to take at what second and how much margin of error is allowed.\n\nHold both grip buttons to start. Complete the sequence as fast and accurately as possible.","gameplayImages":[{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Pull.gif","alt":"Two-handed pull gesture lifting the tyre"},{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Push.gif","alt":"Two-handed push gesture replacing the tyre"},{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Unscrew.png","alt":"Unscrew action with trigger timing"}],"rightBrainNotes":"The F1 car sits in a stylised pit garage with bold red walls and orange/black livery. The wheel mechanism uses concentric rings with colour segments (red outer ring, blue/green/yellow inner ring) providing immediate visual feedback.\n\nWall-mounted text prompts (\"unscrew\", \"pull\", \"success\") appear during gameplay, reinforcing the rhythm-game feel.","artImages":[],"f1Actions":{"pullText":"The Pull action uses a timeline driving a spline-based trajectory: Get Spline Length → Lerp between 0 and spline length → Get Location at Distance Along Spline → Set Relative Location + Rotation on the tyre component. The animation curve ramps linearly for the pull, creating a smooth lift-off.","pushText":"The Push action reverses this process: timeline plays the spline trajectory in reverse, then triggers a sequence — Stop Pull → Set Relative Location (reset tyre) → Set Relative Rotation (reset) → Set Visibility on bolt → Set Actor Location (final position).","detectionText":"The detection pipeline runs on Event Tick:\nGet Component Location → Subtract Vector → Threshold Check → Boolean AND → Branch Node → Increment Counter → Validation Check → IsValid Index? → Collision Check → Trigger Movement","pullImage":{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/PullLine.png","alt":"Spline trajectory for the pull action"},"pushImage":{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/PushLine.png","alt":"Spline trajectory for the push action"},"blueprintImages":[{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Blueprint1.png","alt":"Blueprint - Pull action implementation"},{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Blueprint2.png","alt":"Blueprint - Push action implementation"}]},"f1KeySafeguards":{"description":"Two key safeguards:\n• Temporal Thresholding — An Index > Time check filters out high-frequency noise and unintentional twitches, requiring a minimum of 50ms sustained velocity.\n• Bilateral Validation — Synchronicity checks ensure that both hands act as a unified input source, eliminating asymmetrical misfires and reinforcing the physical \"weight\" of the interaction.","image":{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Array.png","alt":"Keypoint array defining movement types and timing"}},"f1Teleportation":{"description":"Teleportation uses a satisfying arc trace — green for valid NavMesh destinations, red with an X for invalid spots. Long-distance teleportation was disabled for a better gaming experience, keeping the player focused on the pit station.","images":[{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Hands.png","alt":"VR hand controllers in the pit garage"},{"src":"/games/f1-pit-stop-vr/Teleport.png","alt":"Arc teleportation with NavMesh validation"}]},"screenshots":[],"lessons":["What surprised me: The rhythm-based approach made the pit stop feel more like a dance than a chore. Players naturally fell into a flow state after a few attempts.","What I cut: A full pit crew simulation with multiple stations. Focused on perfecting the single-tyre interaction and the feel of the rhythm timing.","What I'd improve: Add haptic feedback patterns for each action type and more varied rhythm patterns across difficulty levels."],"links":[]}]}],["$L4","$L5"],"$L6"]}],"loading":null,"isPartial":false}

6:["$","$L7",null,{"children":["$","$8",null,{"name":"Next.MetadataOutlet","children":"$@9"}]}]
